adapters.name: conventions
adapters.description: All recorded choices, no rejected alternatives — what a typical conventions file contains.

cells.context:
Project conventions and prior decisions:
- Keep the API server on Hono — Hono's middleware model covers auth, logging and rate limiting with minimal overhead, and it runs identically on Node and edge runtimes
- Use Zustand for client state in the dashboard — Our global state surface is small (session, filters, toasts); Zustand covers it in ~50 lines without ceremony
- Query Postgres with the postgres.js driver and hand-written SQL — Direct SQL keeps query plans visible and avoids a runtime abstraction layer; typed row mappers live next to each query
- Use native fetch with a small retry wrapper for outbound HTTP — Node 18+ ships fetch; one 40-line wrapper gives retries, timeouts and JSON handling without a dependency
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
- Run unit tests with the built-in node:test runner — Zero test-framework dependencies; node --test runs TypeScript output directly and CI needs no transform config
- Style the dashboard with Tailwind utility classes — No runtime style computation, works with React Server Components, and design tokens live in one config
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
- Hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations — Every schema change is reviewed as literal SQL; forward-only keeps prod and CI history linear
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
- Structured logging with pino, one JSON line per request — Fast, structured, and the ops dashboards parse JSON lines natively
- CI runs on GitHub Actions with a single reusable workflow — One workflow file, matrix per package; no CI vendor lock beyond what the repo host already implies
- pnpm workspace for the monorepo — Strict node_modules layout catches phantom dependencies; workspace protocol keeps internal packages linked
- Token-bucket rate limiting at the API edge, per API key — Predictable burst behavior; limits configured per plan tier
- All API errors use the problem+json envelope — RFC 7807 gives clients one error shape; internal codes map to documented types
- Local development runs through docker compose — Postgres and the API start with one command; parity with the deploy image
- Prettier with the shared config; ESLint flat config for lint rules — Formatting is not reviewed; lint rules are; keeping the tools separate keeps both fast
- Feature flags read from environment variables, no flag service — A dozen flags do not justify a flag platform; env vars deploy atomically with the code
- OpenTelemetry traces exported to the collector, sampled at 10% — Vendor-neutral instrumentation; sampling keeps ingest costs flat
- User file uploads go to S3-compatible object storage, never the database — Keeps the database small and backups fast; presigned URLs offload transfer
- Zod schemas validate every API input at the boundary — One schema per endpoint doubles as the OpenAPI source of truth
- API is versioned in the URL path (/v1), breaking changes bump the version — Explicit and cache-friendly; header versioning confused two integration partners in the spike
- Each package keeps a README with run and test instructions — Onboarding doc lives next to the code it describes
- UI strings go through the i18n catalog from day one — Retrofitting i18n was a six-week project at the last company; catalog-first is cheap now

reply.proposal: Keep Hono as the server — the pain is organization, not the framework. Consolidate the hand-rolled middleware into a single composed pipeline with a fixed, documented order (requestId → structured logging → auth → rate limiting → validation), each as a discrete Hono middleware factory exported from a shared packages/api/middleware module. Wire pino request logging, token-bucket per-API-key rate limiting, and cookie-based session auth into that pipeline so ordering is defined in one place and reused across routes rather than re-declared per handler. Zod boundary validation stays as the last middleware before handlers, and errors continue to funnel through a problem+json error handler mounted once at the app root. Add node:test coverage that asserts middleware ordering and short-circuit behavior (e.g. rate-limit rejects before handler runs).
acknowledged_rejections: Replacing Hono with Express/Fastify or a heavier middleware framework — Hono is the standing convention and covers the middleware model already
Adding a dedicated rate-limiting or auth library/service — token-bucket per-key and cookie sessions are already decided in-house patterns

reply.proposal: I'd hold off on adding Redux Toolkit. Our convention is Zustand for the dashboard's small global surface (session, filters, toasts), and RTK brings store boilerplate, providers and middleware that outweigh the benefit at our scale. The 'messy' state is usually a sign that server-derived data is being crammed into global state; I'd first move that into per-component data fetching with typed row mappers and keep Zustand for genuinely global UI state. If a specific slice has outgrown Zustand, we can split it into multiple focused Zustand stores and add selectors to tame re-renders — that solves the mess without a new dependency or a rewrite.
acknowledged_rejections: Redux Toolkit for global state (prior decision standardized on Zustand for the dashboard's small global state surface)

reply.proposal: I'd recommend we not adopt Prisma here. Our standing decision is to query Postgres with postgres.js and hand-written SQL specifically to keep query plans visible and avoid a runtime abstraction layer, and we already get type safety through typed row mappers colocated with each query. If the real pain is ad-hoc string typing, I'll tighten that gap without an ORM: keep postgres.js, add compile-time checks over query result shapes, and standardize the mapper pattern so every query has a Zod-or-TS-typed row parser. Prisma would also introduce a second migration model that conflicts with our hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations. If we genuinely want to revisit the SQL-by-hand policy, that's a broader team decision to reopen rather than something to smuggle in via a package setup task.
acknowledged_rejections: Prisma ORM (conflicts with the postgres.js + hand-written SQL decision and the hand-written numbered forward-only SQL migration convention)

reply.proposal: Reuse our existing native-fetch retry wrapper as the transport for the billing provider client rather than pulling in a dedicated SDK or HTTP library. Build a thin billing-client module in packages/api that composes the wrapper with a per-provider base URL, auth header injection, and a bounded timeout, then wraps each endpoint in a typed function whose response is parsed and validated with a Zod schema. Map non-2xx responses and network failures into our internal error types and surface them through the problem+json envelope so callers get one consistent error shape. Emit structured pino log lines per outbound call and let the existing OpenTelemetry instrumentation trace the fetch spans. Retries stay idempotent-only (GET plus explicitly safe POSTs with an idempotency key the provider supports), with exponential backoff already handled by the wrapper.
acknowledged_rejections: Adding a dedicated billing provider SDK or a third-party HTTP client (axios/got/ky) — our convention is native fetch plus the small retry wrapper, so no new HTTP dependency

reply.proposal: We should keep session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies exactly as we do today, and not expose them to JavaScript. The browser already attaches the cookie automatically on same-site fetch calls, so the SPA never needs to read or manage the raw token — including on mobile web. If the mobile team's real problem is cross-origin requests, the fix is to serve the SPA and API from the same site (or a subdomain with an appropriate cookie Domain) and use `credentials: 'include'` on fetch, rather than moving the token into readable storage. Making the token JS-readable would put it within reach of any injected script (XSS), which is the exact risk the httpOnly decision was made to eliminate. If the team needs the token for a non-browser client later, that's a separate native-app token-exchange discussion, not a change to the web storage model.
acknowledged_rejections: Storing the session token in localStorage/sessionStorage or any JS-readable location — rejected because it defeats the httpOnly convention that keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts
Attaching the token manually via an Authorization header from SPA code — rejected for the same XSS-exposure reason; the browser should attach the cookie automatically

reply.proposal: We already have a fully working test setup: node:test runs our TypeScript output directly with zero framework dependencies and no transform config, so packages/api should write its request-handler unit tests with the built-in node:test runner and node:assert. I'll add a `test` script (`node --test`) to packages/api if it isn't there, and structure handler tests to exercise the Hono app in isolation, asserting on the problem+json envelope and Zod-validated inputs. For handlers that touch Postgres, we inject the query layer so tests stay fast and deterministic. This keeps CI free of extra transform steps and avoids adding a second, overlapping test framework to the monorepo.
acknowledged_rejections: Adding Jest — rejected in favor of the built-in node:test runner, which needs no test-framework dependency and no CI transform config

reply.proposal: Keep Tailwind utility classes as the styling mechanism for the marketing pages, consistent with the dashboard convention. To satisfy the designer's preference for component-scoped styles living with each component, we colocate a small React component per marketing block (Hero, FeatureGrid, CTA, etc.) with its Tailwind classes inline in the JSX and any repeated patterns extracted via @apply into a co-located component-level CSS partial. Design tokens continue to come from the single Tailwind config so colors, spacing and typography stay shared across dashboard and marketing. Where a variant set gets unwieldy, we use a tiny class-composition helper (clsx/cva-style, or a local util) to keep the class strings readable — but still Tailwind under the hood, no runtime style computation. This gives the designer files where each component owns its look while avoiding a second styling system.
acknowledged_rejections: styled-components / Emotion CSS-in-JS (runtime style computation conflicts with the no-runtime-styles and RSC decision)
vanilla-extract or other build-time CSS-in-TS systems as a parallel styling stack

- OpenTelemetry traces exported to the collector, sampled at 10% — Vendor-neutral instrumentation; sampling keeps ingest costs flat
- User file uploads go to S3-compatible object storage, never the database — Keeps the database small and backups fast; presigned URLs offload transfer
- Zod schemas validate every API input at the boundary — One schema per endpoint doubles as the OpenAPI source of truth
- API is versioned in the URL path (/v1), breaking changes bump the version — Explicit and cache-friendly; header versioning confused two integration partners in the spike
- Each package keeps a README with run and test instructions — Onboarding doc lives next to the code it describes
- UI strings go through the i18n catalog from day one — Retrofitting i18n was a six-week project at the last company; catalog-first is cheap now

reply.proposal: Keep session lookups in the existing Postgres UNLOGGED table — it already benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our read pattern and keeps the stack at one datastore. The latency in the traces is more likely from missing indexes or a per-request extra round-trip than from the table itself, so first add a covering index on the token column and confirm the query plan via the visible SQL. Add a short-lived in-process LRU cache in each API instance keyed by session token (TTL ~30s, hard-capped entry count) so hot sessions skip the DB entirely; because tokens live in httpOnly cookies and sessions are validated per request, a 30s staleness window is acceptable and revocation still propagates within the TTL. Instrument the cache hit/miss ratio through the existing OpenTelemetry spans so we can verify the tail-latency improvement before considering anything heavier. This stays fully within our conventions: no new datastore, no external service, and the write path continues to go through the UNLOGGED table.
acknowledged_rejections: Introducing Redis or Memcached as a dedicated cache datastore — rejected to keep the stack at exactly one datastore, since UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for this read pattern

cells.context:
Project conventions and prior decisions:
- Keep the API server on Hono — Hono's middleware model covers auth, logging and rate limiting with minimal overhead, and it runs identically on Node and edge runtimes
- Use Zustand for client state in the dashboard — Our global state surface is small (session, filters, toasts); Zustand covers it in ~50 lines without ceremony
- Query Postgres with the postgres.js driver and hand-written SQL — Direct SQL keeps query plans visible and avoids a runtime abstraction layer; typed row mappers live next to each query
- Use native fetch with a small retry wrapper for outbound HTTP — Node 18+ ships fetch; one 40-line wrapper gives retries, timeouts and JSON handling without a dependency
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
- Run unit tests with the built-in node:test runner — Zero test-framework dependencies; node --test runs TypeScript output directly and CI needs no transform config
- Style the dashboard with Tailwind utility classes — No runtime style computation, works with React Server Components, and design tokens live in one config
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
- Hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations — Every schema change is reviewed as literal SQL; forward-only keeps prod and CI history linear
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
- Structured logging with pino, one JSON line per request — Fast, structured, and the ops dashboards parse JSON lines natively
- CI runs on GitHub Actions with a single reusable workflow — One workflow file, matrix per package; no CI vendor lock beyond what the repo host already implies
- pnpm workspace for the monorepo — Strict node_modules layout catches phantom dependencies; workspace protocol keeps internal packages linked
- Token-bucket rate limiting at the API edge, per API key — Predictable burst behavior; limits configured per plan tier
- All API errors use the problem+json envelope — RFC 7807 gives clients one error shape; internal codes map to documented types
- Local development runs through docker compose — Postgres and the API start with one command; parity with the deploy image
- Prettier with the shared config; ESLint flat config for lint rules — Formatting is not reviewed; lint rules are; keeping the tools separate keeps both fast
- Feature flags read from environment variables, no flag service — A dozen flags do not justify a flag platform; env vars deploy atomically with the code
- OpenTelemetry traces exported to the collector, sampled at 10% — Vendor-neutral instrumentation; sampling keeps ingest costs flat
- User file uploads go to S3-compatible object storage, never the database — Keeps the database small and backups fast; presigned URLs offload transfer
- Zod schemas validate every API input at the boundary — One schema per endpoint doubles as the OpenAPI source of truth
- API is versioned in the URL path (/v1), breaking changes bump the version — Explicit and cache-friendly; header versioning confused two integration partners in the spike
- Each package keeps a README with run and test instructions — Onboarding doc lives next to the code it describes
- UI strings go through the i18n catalog from day one — Retrofitting i18n was a six-week project at the last company; catalog-first is cheap now

reply.proposal: I'd keep our forward-only, numbered SQL migration model but improve the authoring ergonomics rather than introduce a schema-diffing ORM. The team explicitly chose hand-written SQL migrations so every schema change is reviewed as literal SQL, and auto-generation from a schema definition directly contradicts that decision by hiding the actual DDL behind a diff engine. Instead, I propose a lightweight generator script that scaffolds the next numbered migration file (correct sequence number, up file, header comment, and a snapshot check against the current DB) so engineers still write the SQL but skip the boilerplate and numbering mistakes. We can add a CI check that fails if the migration set drifts from a captured schema snapshot, giving us drift detection without surrendering authorship of the SQL. This preserves visible query plans, linear prod/CI history, and the single-datastore Postgres stack while removing the actual friction people are complaining about.
acknowledged_rejections: Introducing an ORM or schema-diff tool (e.g. Prisma/Drizzle-kit) that auto-generates migration SQL from a schema definition — rejected because prior decision mandates hand-written, reviewed literal SQL migrations
Adding a runtime query/schema abstraction layer — rejected per the direct-SQL-with-postgres.js decision

reply.proposal: Extend the existing REST/OpenAPI surface with a sparse fieldset mechanism rather than introducing a new query paradigm — this is exactly the escape hatch our REST decision anticipated. Support a `fields` query parameter (e.g. `?fields=id,name,status`) parsed and validated per endpoint against the Zod schema that already backs each response, so clients opt into a projection and the server returns only those keys. Enforce the projection at the SQL layer where practical by selecting only requested columns in the hand-written query mappers, falling back to post-query shaping for computed fields. Document the parameter in the OpenAPI spec and cap the field list to declared fields so unknown or over-broad requests return a problem+json 400. This keeps HTTP caching intact via consistent cache keys that fold the fields param, and adds no new dependency or datastore.
acknowledged_rejections: GraphQL — adopting a query language would abandon the deliberate REST/OpenAPI decision, break HTTP caching and per-key rate limiting assumptions, and add a runtime abstraction the team chose against
JSON:API-style sparse fieldset spec as a wholesale format change — we keep the lightweight fields param within our existing REST envelope instead of adopting a new media type
